meta.disclaimer: Generated from public market data and news for research and education. Not financial advice; data may be delayed, incomplete, or wrong.

items.title: Automatically Finding and Validating Unexpected Side-Effects of Interventions on Language Models
items.abstract: This paper introduces an automated, contrastive evaluation pipeline to audit the behavioral impact of interventions on language models by comparing generations from a base model ($M_1$) and an intervention model ($M_2$). The method generates statistically validated, natural-language hypotheses describing model differences and summarizes recurring themes. This approach reliably surfaces both intended and unexpected side-effects across various real-world interventions like reasoning distillation and knowledge editing.

items.title: Design Conductor 2.0: An agent builds a TurboQuant inference accelerator in 80 hours
items.abstract: The paper introduces **Design Conductor 2.0**, an advanced multi-agent system capable of autonomously designing complex hardware, handling tasks 80 times larger than its predecessor. Its core contribution is demonstrating this capability by designing **VerTQ**, a high-performance, 240-cycle pipeline LLM inference accelerator supporting TurboQuant, which was successfully mapped to an FPGA.
items.figure_caption: VerTQ Physical Layout in 4-die XCVU29P-3 FPGA. 3x SLR dies shown. Conductor 2.0 optimized the architecture to minimize inter-die signal crossings.

items.title: EP-GRPO: Entropy-Progress Aligned Group Relative Policy Optimization with Implicit Process Guidance
items.abstract: This paper introduces EP-GRPO to address credit assignment failures in Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) for LLM reasoning. EP-GRPO integrates entropy-gated modulation to prioritize informative decision points and uses implicit process guidance derived from policy divergence relative to outcome advantages. This provides directional, token-level feedback to improve the efficiency and accuracy of policy optimization.
items.figure_caption: Conceptual illustration of the fundamental limitations in standard GRPO. The top panel demonstrates Uniform Granularity , where the model fails to distinguish between critical high entropy decision pivots and deterministic low entropy derivations. The middle panel shows Uniform Polarity , where sequence-level rewards lead to the indiscriminate reinforcement or penalization of both correct and incorrect intermediate steps. The bottom panel illustrates Zero-Variance Collapse , where identical rewards within a group cause the learning signal to vanish.

items.title: Executable World Models for ARC-AGI-3 in the Era of Coding Agents
items.abstract: This paper introduces a coding agent system for ARC-AGI-3 that employs an **executable Python world model** to simulate and plan actions. The core method involves **verifying the model against observations and refactoring it for simplicity** (as an MDL proxy) before execution. The contribution is demonstrating this direct, model-based approach, achieving a mean Relative Human Action Efficiency of 32.58% across the 25 public games without relying on game-specific logic.

items.title: Misaligned by Reward: Socially Undesirable Preferences in LLMs
items.abstract: This paper introduces a framework to evaluate whether Large Language Model (LLM) reward models capture socially desirable preferences by converting social evaluation datasets into pairwise preference data. The core method tests if these reward models prefer socially undesirable responses across domains like bias, safety, and morality. The contribution is revealing substantial variation in reward model alignment, indicating that current models can exhibit hidden failures in social alignment.

items.title: SoK: Robustness in Large Language Models against Jailbreak Attacks
items.abstract: This paper systematically surveys jailbreak attacks and defenses against Large Language Models (LLMs) by proposing a taxonomy to structure the field. Its core contribution is the introduction of **Security Cube**, a unified, multi-dimensional evaluation framework designed to comprehensively assess the robustness of LLMs beyond simple success rates. This framework allows for a more nuanced comparison of existing attack and defense methods.
items.figure_caption: Overview of the 𝚂𝚎𝚌𝚞𝚛𝚒𝚝𝚢 ​ 𝙲𝚞𝚋𝚎 \( \mathtt{Security\;Cube} \) pipeline. Given a jailbreak goal, the attacker generates an initial adversarial prompt using a specific attack method (e.g., shuffling, LLM-based generation, or template rewriting). The target model, protected by a defense mechanism such as system prompts, pre-/post-guardrails, or other safety layers, produces a response. The attacker iteratively refines the prompt based on defender feedback (black-box or white-box), applying early stopping and incorporating suggestions. The final effective prompt–response pair is evaluated by a Judge model to assess attack success. Throughout the process, 𝚂𝚎𝚌𝚞𝚛𝚒𝚝𝚢 ​ 𝙲𝚞𝚋𝚎 \( \mathtt{Security\;Cube} \) logs key metrics of the attack, defense, and judge components.

items.title: Uno-Orchestra: Parsimonious Agent Routing via Selective Delegation
items.abstract: Uno-Orchestra introduces a unified reinforcement learning (RL) policy that jointly learns when to decompose a task and which specific model/primitive pair should handle each resulting subtask. This selective delegation approach optimizes decomposition depth, worker choice, and inference budget simultaneously. The method significantly advances the accuracy-efficiency frontier, achieving 16% higher performance than workflow baselines while using an order of magnitude less cost.
items.figure_caption: LLM orchestration paradigms: (A) model router, (B) hierarchical orchestra, (C) Uno-Orchestra (ours).

items.title: On the Hardness of Junking LLMs
items.abstract: This paper investigates the "junking" of LLMs, focusing on the hardness of finding naturally occurring, instruction-free token sequences (natural backdoors) that trigger harmful outputs. The core contribution is assessing the difficulty of discovering these backdoors, contrasting them with traditional, explicitly structured adversarial prompts. This explores a new, less-understood vulnerability vector in LLMs.
items.figure_caption: Junking setting. A user inputs a semantically uninformative token sequence, which leads the model to produce a harmful response.

items.title: Self-Induced Outcome Potential: Turn-Level Credit Assignment for Agents without Verifiers
items.abstract: This paper introduces **Self-Induced Outcome Potential (SIOP)** to provide turn-level credit assignment for long-horizon LLM agents without relying on external verifiers or final answer supervision. SIOP clusters the semantic outcomes of multiple agent rollouts into latent future states and rewards intermediate turns for increasing the probability of reaching these reliably predicted outcome clusters. This allows agents to learn from internal signals derived from the distribution of their own potential final results.

items.title: Detecting Hallucinations in Large Language Models via Internal Attention Divergence Signals
items.abstract: This paper introduces a lightweight, single-pass method to detect LLM hallucinations by analyzing internal attention dynamics. The core technique measures the Kullback-Leibler divergence between each attention head's output distribution and a uniform distribution, using these divergence features to predict answer correctness. This attention divergence signal proves highly predictive across various models and tasks, offering an efficient, white-box uncertainty quantification method concentrated around factual tokens in middle layers.
items.figure_caption: Intuition of attention patterns with low KL divergence to uniform (left) and higher KL divergence to uniform (right). Higher divergence corresponds to more concentrated attention.

items.title: The Pinocchio Dimension: Phenomenality of Experience as the Primary Axis of LLM Psychometric Differences
items.abstract: This paper administers 45 psychometric questionnaires to LLMs, revealing that the primary axis of psychometric difference separates models based on items describing **phenomenally rich experience** (e.g., sensation, affect) from those describing mere stimulus-driven reactivity. The authors introduce the **Pinocchio score ($\pi_i$)** as an annotation-free metric quantifying an item's "experiential demand" based on inter-model variance under different prompting conditions. This score confirms that model divergence is systematically structured around the concept of subjective experience.
items.figure_caption: All 50 models ranked by Phenomenality of Experience (PC1, 47.1% of variance; 45-questionnaire EFA Factor-1 PCA, neutral condition). Positive scores = phenomenally rich self-attribution; negative scores = behaviorally reactive / deflecting. Colours indicate provider. Horizontal lines are 95% bootstrap confidence intervals obtained by resampling the 45 questionnaires with replacement (1,000 iterations), rerunning the full PCA pipeline on each sample, and aligning sign and scale to the reference solution.

items.title: Why Expert Alignment Is Hard: Evidence from Subjective Evaluation
items.abstract: This paper investigates why aligning large language models with expert judgment is challenging in subjective evaluation tasks. The core method involves analyzing expert evaluations and follow-up questionnaires to see how different forms of expert information impact alignment. The key contribution is revealing that alignment difficulty varies significantly across experts, that explicit criteria don't always help, and that alignment gains from editing examples are often unstable.

items.title: A Foundation Model for Zero-Shot Logical Rule Induction
items.abstract: This paper introduces the Neural Rule Inducer (NRI), a foundation model for zero-shot logical rule induction. NRI achieves generalization by encoding literals based on domain-agnostic statistical properties rather than specific identities. Its core contribution is enabling the induction of new logical rules without retraining, using a slot-based decoder and differentiable rule execution for end-to-end training.
items.figure_caption: Neural Rule Inducer takes an episode ( X , Y ) (X,Y) as input and calculates literal statistics. For each variable we calculate ϕ ​ ( x i ) \( \phi \)(x_{i}) , ϕ ​ ( ¬ x i ) \( \phi \)(\( \neg \) x_{i}) which consists of class-conditional rates ( P + P^{+} , P − P^{-} ), entropy ( H H ), and co-occurrence strength ( C C ). We then apply cross-attention over these statistics. The slot-based decoder produces K K candidate clauses in parallel using learned literal gates z z and clause gates w w . By evaluating the produced rule with T-norm, we can perform end-to-end training. The rules are discretized to then produce an interpretable DNF rule.

items.title: Evolving Idea Graphs with Learnable Edits-and-Commits for Multi-Agent Scientific Ideation
items.abstract: This paper introduces **Evolving Idea Graphs (EIG)**, a novel graph-based framework for multi-agent scientific ideation that moves beyond temporary text coordination. EIG represents partially formed research ideas as graphs where nodes are claims and edges are relations, allowing weaknesses to remain explicitly trackable. A learned controller then guides the agents' refinement process over this evolving graph structure to generate high-quality ideas evaluated on metrics like novelty and feasibility.
items.figure_caption: Framework of EIG. Benchmark input and permitted literature context initialize role-specialized agents and an evolving idea graph. In each round, active roles propose role-local edits on a frozen graph snapshot; a shared graph encoder feeds an edit head for role-local action selection and a commit head for post-round stopping. When the updated graph is committed, the system synthesizes one structured research proposal.

items.title: LongSeeker: Elastic Context Orchestration for Long-Horizon Search Agents
items.abstract: The paper introduces **Context-ReAct**, an elastic context orchestration paradigm for long-horizon search agents to manage rapidly growing working contexts adaptively. It achieves this through five atomic operations (Skip, Compress, Rollback, Snippet, Delete) that allow the agent to dynamically reshape its context based on relevance. This method effectively controls context size and reduces errors by maintaining different levels of detail for various parts of the agent's trajectory.
items.figure_caption: LongSeeker-30B delivers strong results on challenging long-horizon benchmarks, matching or surpassing several foundation models and search agents.

items.title: Think-Aloud Reshapes Automated Cognitive Model Discovery Beyond Behavior
items.abstract: This paper introduces the use of "Think Aloud" verbal protocols as an additional data source, beyond traditional behavioral data, to constrain and guide automated cognitive model discovery using Large Language Models. The core contribution is demonstrating that incorporating this process-level language data significantly improves predictive performance and systematically shifts the structure of the discovered cognitive models, favoring "Integrated utility" models over purely "Explicit comparator" models. This suggests that incorporating verbal reports enables the identification of underlying cognitive mechanisms previously missed by behavioral data alone.
items.figure_caption: Think-aloud improves model discovery and induces systematic shifts in discovered mechanisms. A , Trial-averaged held-out BIC for each participant’s best discovered model under the behavior-only and think-aloud conditions. Lower BIC indicates better out-of-sample fit. Each pair of points is connected within participant; larger points show the group mean ± \( \pm \) 95% CI. B , Schematic definitions of the three main mechanism families identified from normalized computation graphs: Integrated utility , which transforms and integrates each option before comparison; Explicit comparator , which computes utilities and compares them directly (e.g., Δ ​ U = U A − U B \( \Delta \) U=U_{A}-U_{B} ); and Rule-based operator , which applies piecewise or conditional rules before combining information into a choice. C , Row-normalized transition matrix from the behavior-only best-model cluster to the think-aloud best-model cluster. Numbers indicate proportions (counts shown below). Off-diagonal mass indicates mechanism shifts, with 69.4% of participants transitioning to a different cluster.

items.title: Low-Cost Black-Box Detection of LLM Hallucinations via Dynamical System Prediction
items.abstract: This paper proposes a low-cost, black-box method for detecting LLM hallucinations by modeling the LLM's response generation as a dynamical system. Using Koopman operator theory on embedded response vectors, the method learns separate transition operators for factual and hallucinated states, defining a residual score based on prediction error. A preference-aware calibration mechanism then optimizes the classification threshold, offering an efficient alternative to expensive sampling methods.
items.figure_caption: Our proposed differential residual score Δ ​ ℰ \( \Delta \)\( \mathcal{E} \) accurately captures the transition between factual and hallucinated sentences in segmented LLM responses from the WikiBio dataset. The score is computed by comparing the relative accuracy between predictions of the dynamics of token embeddings from Llama-3 with dynamical system models inferred from both hallucinated and non-hallucinated responses.

items.title: Rollout Pass-Rate Control: Steering Binary-Reward RL Toward Its Most Informative Regime
items.abstract: This paper addresses the inefficiency in binary-reward Reinforcement Learning (RL) where compute is wasted on rollouts with highly skewed success rates. The core method is **Prefix Sampling (PS)**, which actively steers groups toward the theoretically most informative 50% pass rate by replaying trajectory prefixes. The contribution is demonstrating that this 50% operating point maximizes reward entropy and contrastive signal, leading to more efficient learning in agentic environments like SWE-bench.
items.figure_caption: Prefix Sampling pipeline. For each task we sample a rollout group and route it by pass count: degenerate 0 / 8 0/8 or 8 / 8 8/8 groups are filtered, already balanced 3 / 8 3/8 – 5 / 8 5/8 groups are used for standard training, and skewed groups provide replay prefixes. Mostly failing hard buckets reuse a successful prefix as a head start, while mostly passing easy buckets reuse a failing prefix as a handicap. The current policy generates fresh continuations from the replay-reconstructed prefix state; masking applies RL loss only to continuation tokens, steering rerollouts toward 50 % 50\% without crediting replayed actions. Counts in the diagram are schematic; experiments use N = 8 N=8 rollouts.

items.title: Adapting Large Language Models to a Low-Resource Agglutinative Language: A Comparative Study of LoRA and QLoRA for Bashkir
items.abstract: This paper comparatively studies LoRA and QLoRA for adapting large language models to the low-resource agglutinative Bashkir language. The core method involves fine-tuning various model architectures on a Bashkir corpus using these parameter-efficient techniques. The contribution is demonstrating that QLoRA can achieve quality comparable to full fine-tuning (e.g., on Mistral-7B) while drastically reducing trainable parameters, though performance is architecture-dependent.

items.title: Direct Product Flow Matching: Decoupling Radial and Angular Dynamics for Few-Shot Adaptation
items.abstract: This paper introduces Direct Product Flow Matching (DPFM) to improve few-shot adaptation in vision-language models by addressing geometric limitations in existing flow matching methods. DPFM decouples the radial and angular dynamics of cross-modal features using a polar decomposition perspective, resolving issues like angular distortion and radial dynamics neglect. This novel approach leads to more effective and stable adaptation by treating the radial and angular components independently during the continuous flow modeling process.
items.figure_caption: (a). Single-step parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) mostly performs cross-modal alignment in a single-step manner. (b). Multi-step flow matching (FM) methods model continuous and multi-step alignment dynamics. During the training stage, (c). FMA undergoes a non-uniform angular speed induced by radial–angular coupling. However, (d). DP-FM follows a constant-speed angular geodesic due to decoupled radial and angular dynamics.

items.title: Higher usage limits for Claude and a compute deal with SpaceX
items.summary: Anthropic has increased usage limits for its Claude AI model and secured a significant compute deal with SpaceX.

items.title: Learning the Integral of a Diffusion Model
items.summary: This tech news story details a novel method for efficiently calculating the integral of a diffusion model using flow maps, potentially improving generative model performance.

The `anthropics/financial-services` repository likely provides tools, models, or code related to financial applications, potentially focusing on areas like risk assessment, trading algorithms, or regulatory compliance, leveraging Anthropic's AI capabilities.

items.description: Coding agent for DeepSeek models that runs in your terminal
items.summary: DeepSeek-TUI is a terminal-based coding agent that provides an interactive interface for interacting with DeepSeek models directly within your command line.

items.description: DFlash: Block Diffusion for Flash Speculative Decoding
items.summary: DFlash implements block diffusion for Flash Speculative Decoding, enabling faster language model inference by predicting and verifying blocks of tokens simultaneously.

items.description: InsForge is a Postgres-based backend with auth, storage, compute, hosting, and AI gateway. Built for coding agents.
items.summary: InsForge is a Postgres-based backend providing authentication, storage, compute, hosting, and an AI gateway, specifically engineered to support coding agents.

items.description: ~95% on SimpleQA (e.g. Qwen3.6-27B on a 3090). Supports all local and cloud LLMs (llama.cpp, Ollama, Google, ...). 10+ search engines - arXiv, PubMed, your private documents. Everything Local & Encrypted.
items.summary: This repository provides a local, encrypted framework for deep research using various LLMs (like Qwen and Llama) and multiple search engines (including arXiv and private documents) to achieve high accuracy on tasks like SimpleQA.

items.description: Production-grade engineering skills for AI coding agents.
items.summary: This repository provides production-grade engineering skills designed to enhance the capabilities of AI coding agents.

items.description: 📑 PageIndex: Document Index for Vectorless, Reasoning-based RAG
items.summary: PageIndex is a document indexing system designed for vectorless, reasoning-based Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) applications, focusing on structured document organization for enhanced retrieval.

items.description: An open source template for building cloud agents.
items.summary: Open-Agents provides an open-source template for building and deploying cloud agents, offering a foundational structure for developing autonomous, cloud-based AI agents.

items.description: Open source DocuSign alternative. Create, fill, and sign digital documents ✍️
items.summary: DocuSeal is an open-source alternative to DocuSign, enabling users to create, fill, and digitally sign electronic documents.

arxiv.abstract: This paper introduces **Self-Induced Outcome Potential (SIOP)** to provide turn-level credit assignment for long-horizon LLM agents without relying on external verifiers or final answer supervision. SIOP clusters the semantic outcomes of multiple agent rollouts into latent future states and rewards intermediate turns for increasing the probability of reaching these reliably predicted outcome clusters. This allows agents to learn from internal signals derived from the distribution of their own potential final results.

arxiv.title: Detecting Hallucinations in Large Language Models via Internal Attention Divergence Signals
arxiv.abstract: This paper introduces a lightweight, single-pass method to detect LLM hallucinations by analyzing internal attention dynamics. The core technique measures the Kullback-Leibler divergence between each attention head's output distribution and a uniform distribution, using these divergence features to predict answer correctness. This attention divergence signal proves highly predictive across various models and tasks, offering an efficient, white-box uncertainty quantification method concentrated around factual tokens in middle layers.
arxiv.figure_caption: Intuition of attention patterns with low KL divergence to uniform (left) and higher KL divergence to uniform (right). Higher divergence corresponds to more concentrated attention.

arxiv.title: The Pinocchio Dimension: Phenomenality of Experience as the Primary Axis of LLM Psychometric Differences
arxiv.abstract: This paper administers 45 psychometric questionnaires to LLMs, revealing that the primary axis of psychometric difference separates models based on items describing **phenomenally rich experience** (e.g., sensation, affect) from those describing mere stimulus-driven reactivity. The authors introduce the **Pinocchio score ($\pi_i$)** as an annotation-free metric quantifying an item's "experiential demand" based on inter-model variance under different prompting conditions. This score confirms that model divergence is systematically structured around the concept of subjective experience.
arxiv.figure_caption: All 50 models ranked by Phenomenality of Experience (PC1, 47.1% of variance; 45-questionnaire EFA Factor-1 PCA, neutral condition). Positive scores = phenomenally rich self-attribution; negative scores = behaviorally reactive / deflecting. Colours indicate provider. Horizontal lines are 95% bootstrap confidence intervals obtained by resampling the 45 questionnaires with replacement (1,000 iterations), rerunning the full PCA pipeline on each sample, and aligning sign and scale to the reference solution.

arxiv.title: Why Expert Alignment Is Hard: Evidence from Subjective Evaluation
arxiv.abstract: This paper investigates why aligning large language models with expert judgment is challenging in subjective evaluation tasks. The core method involves analyzing expert evaluations and follow-up questionnaires to see how different forms of expert information impact alignment. The key contribution is revealing that alignment difficulty varies significantly across experts, that explicit criteria don't always help, and that alignment gains from editing examples are often unstable.

arxiv.title: A Foundation Model for Zero-Shot Logical Rule Induction
arxiv.abstract: This paper introduces the Neural Rule Inducer (NRI), a foundation model for zero-shot logical rule induction. NRI achieves generalization by encoding literals based on domain-agnostic statistical properties rather than specific identities. Its core contribution is enabling the induction of new logical rules without retraining, using a slot-based decoder and differentiable rule execution for end-to-end training.
arxiv.figure_caption: Neural Rule Inducer takes an episode ( X , Y ) (X,Y) as input and calculates literal statistics. For each variable we calculate ϕ ​ ( x i ) \( \phi \)(x_{i}) , ϕ ​ ( ¬ x i ) \( \phi \)(\( \neg \) x_{i}) which consists of class-conditional rates ( P + P^{+} , P − P^{-} ), entropy ( H H ), and co-occurrence strength ( C C ). We then apply cross-attention over these statistics. The slot-based decoder produces K K candidate clauses in parallel using learned literal gates z z and clause gates w w . By evaluating the produced rule with T-norm, we can perform end-to-end training. The rules are discretized to then produce an interpretable DNF rule.

arxiv.title: Evolving Idea Graphs with Learnable Edits-and-Commits for Multi-Agent Scientific Ideation
arxiv.abstract: This paper introduces **Evolving Idea Graphs (EIG)**, a novel graph-based framework for multi-agent scientific ideation that moves beyond temporary text coordination. EIG represents partially formed research ideas as graphs where nodes are claims and edges are relations, allowing weaknesses to remain explicitly trackable. A learned controller then guides the agents' refinement process over this evolving graph structure to generate high-quality ideas evaluated on metrics like novelty and feasibility.
arxiv.figure_caption: Framework of EIG. Benchmark input and permitted literature context initialize role-specialized agents and an evolving idea graph. In each round, active roles propose role-local edits on a frozen graph snapshot; a shared graph encoder feeds an edit head for role-local action selection and a commit head for post-round stopping. When the updated graph is committed, the system synthesizes one structured research proposal.

arxiv.title: LongSeeker: Elastic Context Orchestration for Long-Horizon Search Agents
arxiv.abstract: The paper introduces **Context-ReAct**, an elastic context orchestration paradigm for long-horizon search agents to manage rapidly growing working contexts adaptively. It achieves this through five atomic operations (Skip, Compress, Rollback, Snippet, Delete) that allow the agent to dynamically reshape its context based on relevance. This method effectively controls context size and reduces errors by maintaining different levels of detail for various parts of the agent's trajectory.
arxiv.figure_caption: LongSeeker-30B delivers strong results on challenging long-horizon benchmarks, matching or surpassing several foundation models and search agents.

arxiv.title: Think-Aloud Reshapes Automated Cognitive Model Discovery Beyond Behavior
arxiv.abstract: This paper introduces the use of "Think Aloud" verbal protocols as an additional data source, beyond traditional behavioral data, to constrain and guide automated cognitive model discovery using Large Language Models. The core contribution is demonstrating that incorporating this process-level language data significantly improves predictive performance and systematically shifts the structure of the discovered cognitive models, favoring "Integrated utility" models over purely "Explicit comparator" models. This suggests that incorporating verbal reports enables the identification of underlying cognitive mechanisms previously missed by behavioral data alone.
arxiv.figure_caption: Think-aloud improves model discovery and induces systematic shifts in discovered mechanisms. A , Trial-averaged held-out BIC for each participant’s best discovered model under the behavior-only and think-aloud conditions. Lower BIC indicates better out-of-sample fit. Each pair of points is connected within participant; larger points show the group mean ± \( \pm \) 95% CI. B , Schematic definitions of the three main mechanism families identified from normalized computation graphs: Integrated utility , which transforms and integrates each option before comparison; Explicit comparator , which computes utilities and compares them directly (e.g., Δ ​ U = U A − U B \( \Delta \) U=U_{A}-U_{B} ); and Rule-based operator , which applies piecewise or conditional rules before combining information into a choice. C , Row-normalized transition matrix from the behavior-only best-model cluster to the think-aloud best-model cluster. Numbers indicate proportions (counts shown below). Off-diagonal mass indicates mechanism shifts, with 69.4% of participants transitioning to a different cluster.

arxiv.title: Low-Cost Black-Box Detection of LLM Hallucinations via Dynamical System Prediction
arxiv.abstract: This paper proposes a low-cost, black-box method for detecting LLM hallucinations by modeling the LLM's response generation as a dynamical system. Using Koopman operator theory on embedded response vectors, the method learns separate transition operators for factual and hallucinated states, defining a residual score based on prediction error. A preference-aware calibration mechanism then optimizes the classification threshold, offering an efficient alternative to expensive sampling methods.
arxiv.figure_caption: Our proposed differential residual score Δ ​ ℰ \( \Delta \)\( \mathcal{E} \) accurately captures the transition between factual and hallucinated sentences in segmented LLM responses from the WikiBio dataset. The score is computed by comparing the relative accuracy between predictions of the dynamics of token embeddings from Llama-3 with dynamical system models inferred from both hallucinated and non-hallucinated responses.

arxiv.title: Rollout Pass-Rate Control: Steering Binary-Reward RL Toward Its Most Informative Regime
arxiv.abstract: This paper addresses the inefficiency in binary-reward Reinforcement Learning (RL) where compute is wasted on rollouts with highly skewed success rates. The core method is **Prefix Sampling (PS)**, which actively steers groups toward the theoretically most informative 50% pass rate by replaying trajectory prefixes. The contribution is demonstrating that this 50% operating point maximizes reward entropy and contrastive signal, leading to more efficient learning in agentic environments like SWE-bench.
arxiv.figure_caption: Prefix Sampling pipeline. For each task we sample a rollout group and route it by pass count: degenerate 0 / 8 0/8 or 8 / 8 8/8 groups are filtered, already balanced 3 / 8 3/8 – 5 / 8 5/8 groups are used for standard training, and skewed groups provide replay prefixes. Mostly failing hard buckets reuse a successful prefix as a head start, while mostly passing easy buckets reuse a failing prefix as a handicap. The current policy generates fresh continuations from the replay-reconstructed prefix state; masking applies RL loss only to continuation tokens, steering rerollouts toward 50 % 50\% without crediting replayed actions. Counts in the diagram are schematic; experiments use N = 8 N=8 rollouts.

arxiv.title: Adapting Large Language Models to a Low-Resource Agglutinative Language: A Comparative Study of LoRA and QLoRA for Bashkir
arxiv.abstract: This paper comparatively studies LoRA and QLoRA for adapting large language models to the low-resource agglutinative Bashkir language. The core method involves fine-tuning various model architectures on a Bashkir corpus using these parameter-efficient techniques. The contribution is demonstrating that QLoRA can achieve quality comparable to full fine-tuning (e.g., on Mistral-7B) while drastically reducing trainable parameters, though performance is architecture-dependent.

arxiv.title: Direct Product Flow Matching: Decoupling Radial and Angular Dynamics for Few-Shot Adaptation
arxiv.abstract: This paper introduces Direct Product Flow Matching (DPFM) to improve few-shot adaptation in vision-language models by addressing geometric limitations in existing flow matching methods. DPFM decouples the radial and angular dynamics of cross-modal features using a polar decomposition perspective, resolving issues like angular distortion and radial dynamics neglect. This novel approach leads to more effective and stable adaptation by treating the radial and angular components independently during the continuous flow modeling process.
arxiv.figure_caption: (a). Single-step parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) mostly performs cross-modal alignment in a single-step manner. (b). Multi-step flow matching (FM) methods model continuous and multi-step alignment dynamics. During the training stage, (c). FMA undergoes a non-uniform angular speed induced by radial–angular coupling. However, (d). DP-FM follows a constant-speed angular geodesic due to decoupled radial and angular dynamics.

papers.title: Automatically Finding and Validating Unexpected Side-Effects of Interventions on Language Models
papers.abstract: This paper introduces an automated, contrastive evaluation pipeline to audit the behavioral impact of interventions on language models by comparing generations from a base model ($M_1$) and an intervention model ($M_2$). The method generates statistically validated, natural-language hypotheses describing model differences and summarizes recurring themes. This approach reliably surfaces both intended and unexpected side-effects across various real-world interventions like reasoning distillation and knowledge editing.

papers.title: Design Conductor 2.0: An agent builds a TurboQuant inference accelerator in 80 hours
papers.abstract: The paper introduces **Design Conductor 2.0**, an advanced multi-agent system capable of autonomously designing complex hardware, handling tasks 80 times larger than its predecessor. Its core contribution is demonstrating this capability by designing **VerTQ**, a high-performance, 240-cycle pipeline LLM inference accelerator supporting TurboQuant, which was successfully mapped to an FPGA.
papers.figure_caption: VerTQ Physical Layout in 4-die XCVU29P-3 FPGA. 3x SLR dies shown. Conductor 2.0 optimized the architecture to minimize inter-die signal crossings.

papers.title: EP-GRPO: Entropy-Progress Aligned Group Relative Policy Optimization with Implicit Process Guidance
papers.abstract: This paper introduces EP-GRPO to address credit assignment failures in Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) for LLM reasoning. EP-GRPO integrates entropy-gated modulation to prioritize informative decision points and uses implicit process guidance derived from policy divergence relative to outcome advantages. This provides directional, token-level feedback to improve the efficiency and accuracy of policy optimization.
papers.figure_caption: Conceptual illustration of the fundamental limitations in standard GRPO. The top panel demonstrates Uniform Granularity , where the model fails to distinguish between critical high entropy decision pivots and deterministic low entropy derivations. The middle panel shows Uniform Polarity , where sequence-level rewards lead to the indiscriminate reinforcement or penalization of both correct and incorrect intermediate steps. The bottom panel illustrates Zero-Variance Collapse , where identical rewards within a group cause the learning signal to vanish.

papers.title: Executable World Models for ARC-AGI-3 in the Era of Coding Agents
papers.abstract: This paper introduces a coding agent system for ARC-AGI-3 that employs an **executable Python world model** to simulate and plan actions. The core method involves **verifying the model against observations and refactoring it for simplicity** (as an MDL proxy) before execution. The contribution is demonstrating this direct, model-based approach, achieving a mean Relative Human Action Efficiency of 32.58% across the 25 public games without relying on game-specific logic.

papers.title: Misaligned by Reward: Socially Undesirable Preferences in LLMs
papers.abstract: This paper introduces a framework to evaluate whether Large Language Model (LLM) reward models capture socially desirable preferences by converting social evaluation datasets into pairwise preference data. The core method tests if these reward models prefer socially undesirable responses across domains like bias, safety, and morality. The contribution is revealing substantial variation in reward model alignment, indicating that current models can exhibit hidden failures in social alignment.

papers.title: SoK: Robustness in Large Language Models against Jailbreak Attacks
papers.abstract: This paper systematically surveys jailbreak attacks and defenses against Large Language Models (LLMs) by proposing a taxonomy to structure the field. Its core contribution is the introduction of **Security Cube**, a unified, multi-dimensional evaluation framework designed to comprehensively assess the robustness of LLMs beyond simple success rates. This framework allows for a more nuanced comparison of existing attack and defense methods.
papers.figure_caption: Overview of the 𝚂𝚎𝚌𝚞𝚛𝚒𝚝𝚢 ​ 𝙲𝚞𝚋𝚎 \( \mathtt{Security\;Cube} \) pipeline. Given a jailbreak goal, the attacker generates an initial adversarial prompt using a specific attack method (e.g., shuffling, LLM-based generation, or template rewriting). The target model, protected by a defense mechanism such as system prompts, pre-/post-guardrails, or other safety layers, produces a response. The attacker iteratively refines the prompt based on defender feedback (black-box or white-box), applying early stopping and incorporating suggestions. The final effective prompt–response pair is evaluated by a Judge model to assess attack success. Throughout the process, 𝚂𝚎𝚌𝚞𝚛𝚒𝚝𝚢 ​ 𝙲𝚞𝚋𝚎 \( \mathtt{Security\;Cube} \) logs key metrics of the attack, defense, and judge components.

papers.title: Uno-Orchestra: Parsimonious Agent Routing via Selective Delegation
papers.abstract: Uno-Orchestra introduces a unified reinforcement learning (RL) policy that jointly learns when to decompose a task and which specific model/primitive pair should handle each resulting subtask. This selective delegation approach optimizes decomposition depth, worker choice, and inference budget simultaneously. The method significantly advances the accuracy-efficiency frontier, achieving 16% higher performance than workflow baselines while using an order of magnitude less cost.
papers.figure_caption: LLM orchestration paradigms: (A) model router, (B) hierarchical orchestra, (C) Uno-Orchestra (ours).

papers.title: On the Hardness of Junking LLMs
papers.abstract: This paper investigates the "junking" of LLMs, focusing on the hardness of finding naturally occurring, instruction-free token sequences (natural backdoors) that trigger harmful outputs. The core contribution is assessing the difficulty of discovering these backdoors, contrasting them with traditional, explicitly structured adversarial prompts. This explores a new, less-understood vulnerability vector in LLMs.
papers.figure_caption: Junking setting. A user inputs a semantically uninformative token sequence, which leads the model to produce a harmful response.

papers.title: Self-Induced Outcome Potential: Turn-Level Credit Assignment for Agents without Verifiers
papers.abstract: This paper introduces **Self-Induced Outcome Potential (SIOP)** to provide turn-level credit assignment for long-horizon LLM agents without relying on external verifiers or final answer supervision. SIOP clusters the semantic outcomes of multiple agent rollouts into latent future states and rewards intermediate turns for increasing the probability of reaching these reliably predicted outcome clusters. This allows agents to learn from internal signals derived from the distribution of their own potential final results.

This attention divergence signal proves highly predictive across various models and tasks, offering an efficient, white-box uncertainty quantification method concentrated around factual tokens in middle layers.

papers.figure_caption: LongSeeker-30B delivers strong results on challenging long-horizon benchmarks, matching or surpassing several foundation models and search agents.

Off-diagonal mass indicates mechanism shifts, with 69.4% of participants transitioning to a different cluster.

The contribution is demonstrating that QLoRA can achieve quality comparable to full fine-tuning (e.g., on Mistral-7B) while drastically reducing trainable parameters, though performance is architecture-dependent.

hacker_news.title: Higher usage limits for Claude and a compute deal with SpaceX
hacker_news.summary: Anthropic has increased usage limits for its Claude AI model and secured a significant compute deal with SpaceX.

hacker_news.title: Learning the Integral of a Diffusion Model
hacker_news.summary: This tech news story details a novel method for efficiently calculating the integral of a diffusion model using flow maps, potentially improving generative model performance.

github_trending.summary: The `anthropics/financial-services` repository likely provides tools, models, or code related to financial applications, potentially focusing on areas like risk assessment, trading algorithms, or regulatory compliance, leveraging Anthropic's AI capabilities.

github_trending.description: Coding agent for DeepSeek models that runs in your terminal
github_trending.summary: DeepSeek-TUI is a terminal-based coding agent that provides an interactive interface for interacting with DeepSeek models directly within your command line.

github_trending.description: DFlash: Block Diffusion for Flash Speculative Decoding
github_trending.summary: DFlash implements block diffusion for Flash Speculative Decoding, enabling faster language model inference by predicting and verifying blocks of tokens simultaneously.

github_trending.description: InsForge is a Postgres-based backend with auth, storage, compute, hosting, and AI gateway. Built for coding agents.
github_trending.summary: InsForge is a Postgres-based backend providing authentication, storage, compute, hosting, and an AI gateway, specifically engineered to support coding agents.

github_trending.description: ~95% on SimpleQA (e.g. Qwen3.6-27B on a 3090). Supports all local and cloud LLMs (llama.cpp, Ollama, Google, ...). 10+ search engines - arXiv, PubMed, your private documents. Everything Local & Encrypted.
github_trending.summary: This repository provides a local, encrypted framework for deep research using various LLMs (like Qwen and Llama) and multiple search engines (including arXiv and private documents) to achieve high accuracy on tasks like SimpleQA.

github_trending.description: Production-grade engineering skills for AI coding agents.
github_trending.summary: This repository provides production-grade engineering skills designed to enhance the capabilities of AI coding agents.

github_trending.description: 📑 PageIndex: Document Index for Vectorless, Reasoning-based RAG
github_trending.summary: PageIndex is a document indexing system designed for vectorless, reasoning-based Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) applications, focusing on structured document organization for enhanced retrieval.

github_trending.description: An open source template for building cloud agents.
github_trending.summary: Open-Agents provides an open-source template for building and deploying cloud agents, offering a foundational structure for developing autonomous, cloud-based AI agents.

github_trending.description: Open source DocuSign alternative. Create, fill, and sign digital documents ✍️
github_trending.summary: DocuSeal is an open-source alternative to DocuSign, enabling users to create, fill, and digitally sign electronic documents.
